explanation.rule: The prefix dis- is added to the beginning of verbs, adjectives, and nouns to give the opposite meaning or to indicate reversal of an action.

groups.note: Added to words to give the opposite or negative meaning. Simply place dis- before the base word with no spelling changes.

examples.definition: to have a different opinion from someone else
to stop being visible; to go away suddenly
not honest; willing to lie or cheat
to separate or break a connection
to refuse to follow rules or instructions

wordForms.definition: to have a different opinion from someone else
wordForms.example: The two scientists disagree about the cause of the problem.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to have the same opinion as someone else

wordForms.definition: to stop being visible; to go away suddenly
wordForms.example: The magician made the coin disappear in an instant.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to become visible or to arrive

wordForms.definition: to find something unpleasant; to not enjoy something
wordForms.example: She dislikes spicy food so she never orders it.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to find something pleasant or enjoyable

wordForms.definition: to make someone feel sad because something was not as good as expected
wordForms.example: The poor weather disappointed the tourists who had planned a picnic.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to choose someone officially for a role or position

wordForms.definition: to find or learn about something for the first time
wordForms.example: Scientists discovered a new species of fish in the deep ocean.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to place something over in order to protect or hide it

wordForms.definition: not honest; willing to lie, cheat, or deceive
wordForms.example: It is dishonest to copy someone else's work and call it your own.
wordForms.rootDefinition: telling the truth and not cheating or stealing

wordForms.definition: to separate or break a connection between two things
wordForms.example: Please disconnect the charger when the battery is full.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to join or link two things together

wordForms.definition: a state of confusion or untidiness; a medical condition
wordForms.example: The office was in complete disorder after the move.
wordForms.rootDefinition: a structured arrangement; a system of rules

wordForms.definition: a feeling of slight pain or unease
wordForms.example: She felt some discomfort in her shoulder after lifting the heavy box.
wordForms.rootDefinition: a pleasant feeling of ease and relaxation

wordForms.definition: to refuse to follow rules or instructions
wordForms.example: Any soldier who disobeys an order faces serious consequences.

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: I ___ with your opinion — I think the decision was completely wrong.
The suspect managed to ___ into the crowd before police arrived.
It is ___ to take credit for work that someone else has done.
Remember to ___ your devices from the power supply before leaving.
Children who ___ their parents at a young age often cause problems later.
She was ___ by the ending of the film — she had expected something more exciting.

transformExercises.sentence: The two experts ___ on the best way to solve the problem. (AGREE)
The fog was so thick that the mountains seemed to ___ completely. (APPEAR)
The room was in total ___ after the children's party. (ORDER)
Please ___ the cable from the laptop before packing it away. (CONNECT)
She felt some ___ in her leg after the long hike. (COMFORT)
